claimed_contribution: Introduces and empirically documents the "Abercrombie and Fitch effect," where exposure to physically dominant men increases male status-signaling consumption.
Provides one of the first demonstrations of financial consequences of physically dominant male employees and models for male customers' spending behavior.
Shows who is most affected and why by identifying moderators (men's own physical dominance markers) and process evidence (intrasexual competitiveness).
context: The paper studies retail and advertising-relevant settings in which customers are exposed to physically dominant men, motivated by practices at brands such as Abercrombie and Fitch and Hollister. Evidence includes an in-store field setting at a large global furniture retailer and multiple image-based behavioral studies.
design_and_data: A pilot study (N = 380 undergraduates) measured lay beliefs about how athletic/muscular male employees affect male versus female customer spending. Study 1 was a quasi-experimental retail field study (N = 369 customers) manipulating the presence versus absence of a physically dominant male employee at store entry and recording spending from customer reports and receipt photos (including average price per item). Study 2a (N = 114 undergraduates) and Study 2b (N = 292 undergraduates) experimentally manipulated exposure to dominant versus nondominant men and measured logo-size preferences/drawings, with Study 2b testing moderation by participant stature via PROCESS Model 3. Study 3 (N = 473 undergraduates) measured willingness to spend on status-signaling versus functional goods, intrasexual competitiveness, and 2D:4D ratio, and tested moderated mediation with PROCESS Model 9.
main_findings: Contrary to lay theory, a dominant male employee increased male customers' total spending and average price per item relative to female customers in the field study, while no male-female spending difference appeared when the employee was absent.
Exposure to physically dominant versus nondominant men increased men's preference for and drawing of larger logos, with no comparable effect for women.

Effects were strongest among men lower in own physical dominance (shorter stature or higher 2D:4D ratio), and elevated intrasexual competitiveness mediated greater relative willingness to spend on status-signaling rather than functional products.
one_line_summary: Across a field study and experiments, exposure to physically dominant men increased male status-signaling consumption, especially among less physically dominant men, through heightened intrasexual competitiveness.
relation_to_prior_work: extends evolutionary consumer research that has mainly emphasized attractive women as triggers of male conspicuous consumption by showing that physically dominant men can trigger male-to-male status signaling. It challenges consumer lay theory predicting stronger spending effects for female customers in the presence of dominant male employees.
research_gap: Prior research had extensively examined effects of attractive women on male purchase behavior, but less was known about how physically dominant men influence other men's consumption. The paper addresses whether male dominance cues activate intrasexual competition and shift consumption toward status-signaling goods.
research_question: Do physically dominant male employees or models increase male customers' status-signaling consumption relative to female customers? Is this effect stronger among men who are less physically dominant themselves, and is it driven by heightened intrasexual competitiveness?
